response: ## Review verdict: request changes (don't block) **File size from evidence:** "byte size: 260,000 bytes (253.9 KB)" — just over the 200 KB low-severity threshold (high doesn't start until 1000 KB). The finding's severity is `low` with confidence 0.95, so this is a clear, real issue but not a stop-the-line one. Request changes; don't block the PR. ## Recommended transformation **Target dimensions:** Hero banners typically render somewhere around **1440–1600 px wide** on desktop and scale down on mobile. Re-export the source at **2× the largest CSS render width** so retina/HiDPI displays still look crisp: - Single asset: **2880 × ~1200 px** (assuming a ~21:9 or 12:5 hero crop) - Better: ship a responsive `` with `srcset` at 1×/1.5×/2× breakpoints (e.g. 1440w, 2160w, 2880w) so phones don't download desktop bytes The current PNG almost certainly has more pixels than the visible render area needs — that's where most of the 253.9 KB is going. ## Format: AVIF (with WebP fallback) For a **hero banner specifically**, I'd re-encode as **AVIF**: 1. **Compression** — AVIF typically lands 20–50% smaller than WebP at equal perceptual quality, and ~70% smaller than PNG. On a hero, those bytes are the LCP image, so the savings show up directly in Core Web Vitals. 2. **Support** — Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Safari 16+ all decode AVIF natively. The remaining gap (older Safari) is exactly what `` is for. 3. **Why not WebP alone** — WebP is the safe default for photographic content, but on a single, hot, above-the-fold image, AVIF's extra compression is worth the encode-time cost. For decorative thumbnails or icons WebP is fine; for the hero, lean on AVIF. If the toolchain doesn't have AVIF encoding wired up, WebP alone at ~quality 75 will still cut the file to ~50–80 KB and is an acceptable interim fix.
